11f3f8e4eb3dda74f4899de160abfc10.cause_description: The introduction of a Pre-Approval Review Step for International Travel Declarations could suggest a series of incremental changes aimed at enhancing compliance and financial controls within the organization. This modification, which was implemented in response to findings from an external audit, indicates a shift towards more rigorous oversight and iterative improvements in the travel declaration process. The evidence may indicate that such small, continuous adjustments are part of a broader trend of evolving practices in response to identified discrepancies, reflecting an agile approach to process management.
11f3f8e4eb3dda74f4899de160abfc10.evidence_snippet: Subject: Introduction of Pre-Approval Review Step for International Travel Declarations From: Office of Risk & Compliance Date: 15 October 2017 To: Heads of Department, Project Leads, Faculty Coordinators Category: Compliance Directive – Action Required Dear Colleagues, As part of the Q1 compliance update and in response to the findings of the 2017 external audit on research mobility expenditures, we are implementing a structural modification to the international travel declaration process.

27844745c65e4e1669e91bd222a2bbba.cause_description: The implementation of a mandatory data validation protocol for travel permit submissions, as outlined in the communication from the Finance and Compliance Coordination Office, could suggest a series of incremental changes aimed at improving data accuracy and compliance. This gradual enforcement of new requirements, such as the inclusion of a valid budget number and project details, may reflect an iterative approach to process management that aligns with agile practices, where small adjustments are made over time to enhance overall process integrity.
27844745c65e4e1669e91bd222a2bbba.evidence_snippet: Subject: Enforced Metadata Validation for Travel Permit Submissions From: Finance and Compliance Coordination Office Date: 17 October 2017 To: All Budget Owners, Project Leads, Travel Officers Category: Process Enforcement Dear Colleagues, In preparation for the annual financial reconciliation and internal audit season, we are implementing a mandatory data validation protocol on all travel permit submissions, effective 1 November 2017. Policy Update All submitted travel permits must now contain: • A valid budget number • A specified project number and task ID • A populated activity code (if applicable) Permits lacking these fields will be automatically flagged for pre-approval review and may be returned to the requester. Compliance Context This update aligns with Article 9 of the University Financial Control Framework and addresses audit concerns regarding: • Budget mismatches • Duplicate trip registrations • “Unknown” fields in travel cost tracking reports

ebe41ecd65a73d23fdb6be5943d14e1d.cause_description: The evidence may indicate that the university's governance for Requests for Payment (RFPs) has undergone a series of small adjustments aimed at enhancing efficiency and minimizing process friction. The memo outlines a structure that allows for only one supervisor approval for payments under €1,000, which could suggest an incremental approach to streamline the payment process over time. This aligns with the concept of incremental drift, where continuous, minor changes accumulate to create a significant transformation in the process.
ebe41ecd65a73d23fdb6be5943d14e1d.evidence_snippet: This structure is designed to ensure efficiency and minimize process friction for routine expenses. Any changes will be communicated by the Controller’s Office.

5afdf5ef1e103aa288d541036611b462.cause_description: The evidence may indicate that the introduction of a Pre-Approval Review Step for International Travel Declarations represents a gradual transition to a new process variant. This change, effective from May 21, 2018, suggests that while the new pre-approval step is being implemented, ongoing declarations submitted before June 30, 2018, will still be processed under the old system but flagged for review. This coexistence of the old and new processes during the transition period aligns with the characteristics of a gradual drift, where both variants operate simultaneously until the older version is phased out.
